name: Audulomi
dates_notes: Pre-Bādarāyaṇa; cited by name in the Brahma-Sūtra itself (e.g., 1.4.21, 4.4.6); no work survives.
core_thesis: As reconstructed from later citations: Auḍulomi survives only as a name and a position invoked by Bādarāyaṇa at Brahma-Sūtra 1.4.21 and 4.4.6, with Śaṅkara's bhāṣya supplying the standard gloss. The doctrine attributed to him concerns the *jīva-Brahman* relation specifically at the moment of *mokṣa*: the liberated self becomes manifest only as pure consciousness (*caitanya-mātra*), so that the relation of *jīva* and Brahman is one of absolute non-difference (*abheda*) at liberation, whatever distinctions obtained in the embodied state. Bādarāyaṇa appears to endorse this as the upaniṣadic truth concerning the liberated state at 4.4.6 (*caitanyena ātmanā iti auḍulomiḥ*). Nothing else of the historical Auḍulomi is recoverable; the figure functions in the tradition as the name attached to a *mokṣa*-state non-difference thesis later absorbed into Advaita and partially echoed in *bhedābheda* readings of the embodied/liberated distinction.

engaged_works.title: Positions reconstructed from Brahma-Sūtra citations
engaged_works.ascription_notes: No work survives; positions are reconstructed solely from Brahma-Sūtra references (1.4.21; 4.4.6).
engaged_works.summary: Two sūtra-loci preserve Auḍulomi's view. Brahma-Sūtra 1.4.21 cites him on the nature of the *jīva* in relation to Brahman (against Āśmarathya's mediated *bhedābheda* and alongside Kāśakṛtsna's identity-thesis). Brahma-Sūtra 4.4.6 (*caitanyena ātmanā iti auḍulomiḥ*) preserves the most-cited fragment: the liberated self stands forth purely *as* consciousness, the *upādhi*-bound distinctions of embodied life having dropped away. Śaṅkara's bhāṣya treats this as a statement about the liberated state's intrinsic nature, harmonizable with the larger non-difference thesis. Later Advaitins read Auḍulomi as a witness to the *mokṣa*-state identity reading; Viśiṣṭādvaita reads the same locus as compatible with a relational *abheda* preserving qualified distinctions. The historical position behind the citation cannot be recovered beyond this *mokṣa*-state non-difference claim.
